topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the kitchen was a physical weight pressing down on me. Every clink of silverware, every measured sigh from my mother, felt like an intrusion into a carefully constructed solitude. I kept my movements stiff, like a poorly oiled machine, avoiding eye contact at all costs. When my father asked if I wanted help with the groceries, the question landed like an accusation. I just grunted, the sound rougher than I intended, and turned sharply toward the living room, the scrape of my chair against the hardwood floor a deliberate, sharp noise in the quiet room. I focused intensely on the pattern of the wallpaper, willing myself to become invisible.

[story 2]

I traced the grain of the wooden table with my fingertip, the texture rough beneath my skin. The air in the house felt thick, saturated with unspoken expectations. When my sister started talking about her weekend plans, my jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath my skin. I offered only clipped, one-word responses, my voice deliberately low and flat, refusing to engage in any warm exchange. I found myself standing near the doorway, arms crossed tightly over my chest, observing the scene with a detached scrutiny that bordered on disdain. I wanted to retreat entirely, to dissolve into the shadows, leaving this shared space untouched by my presence.

[story 3]

The scent of my mother’s baking, usually comforting, now seemed cloying, suffocating. I moved through the hallway with a practiced economy of motion, minimizing contact, keeping my shoulders hunched forward as if bracing for a blow. When my father attempted to make small talk about the garden, I cut him off mid-sentence, my tone sharp and dismissive. "I'm fine," I stated, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I didn't wait for a reply, simply turning away and walking toward the stairs, the sound of my footsteps echoing too loudly in the cavernous space. I needed distance; the proximity was simply too much to bear.
